title: America, Iran, and a World in Turmoil
gist: In a Foreign Affairs Interview with Dan Kurtz-Phelan, Eurasia Group's Ian Bremmer argues the Iran war was Trump's 'greatest foreign policy failure,' explains why chokepoint leverage (Hormuz, Chinese rare earths) is a self-defeating 'diminishing asset' that accelerates deglobalization and a green-energy build-out, maps a G-Zero world splitting into hedging blocs (a Saudi-Pakistan-Turkey-Egypt axis vs. an Abrahamic UAE-Israel axis), and warns that ungoverned AI now poses systemic risks — bioweapons, drone swarms, infrastructure attacks, financial-market bot fleets — on par with a cornered, nuclear-armed Putin.

themes.theme: Trump's Iran war as strategic failure

insights.claim: Bremmer calls the Iran war Trump's 'greatest foreign policy failure' because Trump achieved none of his direct war aims and wildly miscalculated that Iranian losses would force capitulation, with costs to U.S. allies and the economy that Bremmer rates as worse than the failed Liberation Day tariff confrontation with China.
insights.speaker: Ian Bremmer
insights.so_what: Sets the framing for reading all subsequent U.S. Middle East policy as damage control from a self-inflicted strategic setback, not from a position of restored leverage.
insights.quote: greatest foreign policy failure
insights.locator: Well, let me start with a very simple question

insights.claim: 92 percent of Israelis surveyed believe the Iran war was a failure and that Israel lost, though Bremmer himself characterizes the outcome as more of a 'climb down' than an outright Iranian win, given Iran's assassinated leadership and damaged military/economic infrastructure.
insights.speaker: Ian Bremmer
insights.so_what: A concrete, high-magnitude public-opinion data point that undercuts any narrative of a clean Israeli/U.S. victory — relevant to assessing Netanyahu's and Trump's political capital going forward.
insights.quote: 92 percent of Israelis surveyed believe that this war is a failure, believe that they lost.
insights.locator: including as of this last couple of days

insights.claim: Trump got almost nothing on any of his stated Iran war goals — the nuclear program, missiles, regional proxies, or regime change — and Iran arguably ended up in a stronger near-term position after demonstrating it could 'play much larger adversaries to a draw.'
insights.speaker: Ian Bremmer
insights.so_what: Establishes the baseline for judging any future U.S.-Iran negotiation: Washington is negotiating from a position of demonstrated failure, not strength, which should temper expectations of a favorable nuclear deal.
insights.locator: Yeah, and as you walk through the various goals

insights.claim: Trump publicly said he had to end the Strait of Hormuz blockade or the world would face economic catastrophe and that he did not want to become 'another President Hoover' — an implicit admission that Iran had the capacity to inflict serious economic damage on the U.S., paralleling China's ability to shut down American factories via rare-earth export controls.
insights.speaker: Ian Bremmer
insights.so_what: Identifies a real, tested chokepoint-leverage capability in Iran's hands (and China's) that materially constrains U.S. escalation options — a durable geopolitical risk factor for oil and critical-minerals-exposed positioning.
insights.locator: And it’s very similar to the Chinese having the capacity to shut down American factories

themes.theme: Chokepoint leverage as a diminishing asset

insights.claim: Bremmer's core framework: using geopolitical chokepoint leverage — whether Iran's control of the Strait of Hormuz or China's rare-earth monopoly — makes it a 'diminishing asset,' because its exercise immediately triggers global investment in alternatives that erodes future pricing power.
insights.speaker: Ian Bremmer
insights.so_what: A reusable mental model for pricing any chokepoint-leverage play (oil transit, critical minerals, semiconductors) — the act of weaponizing scarcity is self-limiting and should be discounted as a persistent risk premium.
insights.quote: when you use the leverage, it becomes a diminishing asset
insights.locator: Now, I also think, and this is an important point

insights.claim: Diversification away from Chinese rare-earth dependence is already underway and could close within 12–24 months for some critical minerals, though heavy rare earths would take five to seven years to substitute.
insights.speaker: Ian Bremmer
insights.so_what: Gives investors a rough timeline for when China's rare-earth pricing power meaningfully erodes — informs positioning in non-Chinese critical-minerals capacity plays.
insights.locator: within a period, in some cases and with critical minerals

insights.claim: Immediately after Iran demonstrated its ability to disrupt the Strait of Hormuz, the Emiratis began building a new overland oil pipeline and the region saw new rail and trucking infrastructure built to reduce reliance on the strait.
insights.speaker: Ian Bremmer
insights.so_what: A concrete, near-term infrastructure response confirming the 'diminishing asset' thesis in real time — relevant to regional oil-logistics and pipeline-infrastructure investment.
insights.locator: And immediately after they use it, the Emiratis start building a new pipeline over land

insights.claim: Bremmer expects some form of 'tolling' — insurance fees, environmental fees, management fees, or reconstruction investment into Iran — to persist beyond the 60-day MOU window even after the strait formally reopens, extracted by both Iran and Oman.
insights.speaker: Ian Bremmer
insights.so_what: A specific, monetizable prediction for shippers and energy traders: expect a durable de facto transit toll on Hormuz-bound cargo even after the acute crisis passes.
insights.locator: Insurance, environmental fees, management fees

insights.claim: Bremmer argues that, looking back in five to ten years, Trump could be seen as the U.S. president who did the most to foster the green-energy transition — an unintended second-order effect of the Iran war's disruption to the Strait of Hormuz pushing the world toward EVs, batteries, and smart grids.
insights.speaker: Ian Bremmer
insights.so_what: A genuinely contrarian, non-obvious call: energy-transition and EV/battery investors should treat Middle East chokepoint instability as a structural demand tailwind rather than only a risk factor.
insights.quote: we might see that President Trump has become the U.S. president that has done the most to actually foster and support a transition to green energy than any other president
insights.locator: I mean, I joked the other day

insights.claim: China is in 'pole position' globally on investment at scale in sustainable-energy technologies, meaning the war-driven acceleration toward EVs, batteries, and solar will disproportionately benefit Chinese EV, battery, and solar-panel makers even as China's rare-earth leverage erodes.
insights.speaker: Ian Bremmer
insights.so_what: A direct trade implication: Hormuz-driven green-energy demand growth is a near-term tailwind for Chinese clean-tech manufacturers, partially offsetting China's loss of rare-earth chokepoint leverage.
insights.locator: The Chinese are in pole position globally

insights.claim: The 'incredible voracious appetite' of AI hyperscalers building data centers at unprecedented infrastructure scale is independently driving a surge in U.S. demand for sustainable energy, compounding the Hormuz-driven shift.
insights.speaker: Ian Bremmer
insights.so_what: Identifies AI data-center buildout as a second, independent structural driver of U.S. clean-power demand — relevant to power-generation and grid-infrastructure investment theses tied to AI capex.
insights.quote: the incredible voracious appetite for the hyperscalers in AI
insights.locator: the incredible voracious appetite for the hyperscalers in AI and the data centers

insights.claim: Bremmer argues the world has moved from a Washington-consensus era of 'just-in-time globalization' to a 'G-Zero' world, evidenced by repeated chokepoint failures — Taiwan's semiconductor monopoly, Europe's dependence on Russian gas, China's rare-earth dominance, and now Hormuz oil/gas/LNG/helium/petrochem flows — that argue for decentralized investment, more resilience, and more industrial policy and nationalism.
insights.speaker: Ian Bremmer
insights.so_what: A macro allocation framework: 'just-in-time, single-source' supply chains carry an underpriced geopolitical tail risk; expect continued capital reallocation toward redundant, nationally-diversified supply chains.
insights.locator: We’ve seen it with semiconductors being monopolized by Taiwan

insights.claim: Deglobalization is also being driven by economics independent of politics: rising AI/robotics capability is reducing the need for offshored labor, illustrated by a major bank CEO planning to cut 12,000 jobs in India partly due to the security risk of holding human capital offshore versus using AI with data secured domestically.
insights.speaker: Ian Bremmer
insights.so_what: A concrete, quantified data point on AI directly substituting for offshored labor at a major financial institution — an early indicator for outsourcing/BPO-sector revenue risk in India and similar markets.
insights.quote: removing 12,000 people from India
insights.locator: I was talking to a CEO of a pretty big bank last week

themes.theme: Middle East splits into two hedging coalitions

insights.claim: Bremmer identifies a Saudi Arabia-Pakistan-Turkey-Egypt bloc forming as Riyadh hedges away from reliance on Washington: Pakistan now has 13,000 troops on the ground in Saudi Arabia, direct Pakistani air force presence, and an informal nuclear umbrella.
insights.speaker: Ian Bremmer
insights.so_what: A specific, trackable military-alignment shift — Pakistani troop and air presence in Saudi Arabia signals Riyadh is actively building a non-U.S. security backstop, relevant to Gulf defense and geopolitical-risk positioning.
insights.quote: 13,000 troops on the ground in Pakistan
insights.locator: You got 13,000 troops on the ground in Pakistan

insights.claim: Saudi Arabia's missile program already sources from China because the U.S. will not sell it Tomahawks — concrete evidence Riyadh is hedging toward Beijing even while maintaining a transactional relationship with the Trump administration.
insights.speaker: Ian Bremmer
insights.so_what: A specific, named defense-procurement fact demonstrating Saudi hedging behavior — relevant to assessing U.S. defense-export market share and China's inroads into Gulf security relationships.
insights.quote: whose missile program comes from China because the Americans aren’t going to provide them with Tomahawks
insights.locator: but also the Saudis, whose missile program comes from China

insights.claim: In the rival 'Abrahamic' coalition, the UAE has deepened reliance on Israel after the Iran war and unilaterally withdrew from OPEC, treating its own oil as a 'wasting resource' it wants to maximize exporting now rather than husband for the long term.
insights.speaker: Ian Bremmer
insights.so_what: A specific, contrarian signal on Gulf oil strategy: the UAE's OPEC exit and 'wasting resource' framing implies it intends to keep pumping at scale into a declining long-term price environment rather than restrain output — relevant to OPEC+ cohesion and oil-price forecasting.
insights.quote: they see their own oil as a wasting resource
insights.locator: given the second-order effects on everyone else moving away from oil and gas faster

insights.claim: Bremmer expects the UAE, more a 'global node' akin to Singapore than a regional Arab-Muslim power, to back a continued Israeli 'mowing the grass' strategy against Iran if the 60-day nuclear negotiation fails, rather than pursue the Saudi-style diplomatic accommodation route via Pakistan and China.
insights.speaker: Ian Bremmer
insights.so_what: Forecasts a split Gulf response to any renewed Iran crisis — UAE aligning with continued Israeli military pressure versus Saudi Arabia pursuing negotiated accommodation — a key variable for regional-conflict escalation risk.
insights.quote: mowing the grass strategy
insights.locator: I think that the Emiratis are much more likely to support a mowing the grass strategy

themes.theme: US-Israel relationship: strained but not rupturing

insights.claim: Despite friction over Lebanon and rhetoric from JD Vance, Bremmer does not see this as an inflection point for the U.S.-Israel relationship because of deep integration with U.S. military-industrial-technology companies (Palantir, Anduril, Scale AI) and the domestic political economy of defense spending across all 435 congressional districts.
insights.speaker: Ian Bremmer
insights.so_what: Pushes back against the view (raised elsewhere in Foreign Affairs by Dana Stroul) that joint U.S.-Israel combat in the Iran war damages the alliance — implies continued durability of U.S. defense-tech names tied to Israel regardless of political noise.
insights.locator: Not yet. I don’t. Few things

insights.claim: Bremmer is confident the cease-fire with Iran will hold because Trump has repeatedly issued maximal threats and then backed away, with the only post-cease-fire strike being small and calibrated after Iran downed a U.S. helicopter, signaling reluctance to re-engage in full war.
insights.speaker: Ian Bremmer
insights.so_what: A high-conviction near-term stability call relevant to oil-price and regional-risk premium forecasting — Bremmer explicitly expects the strait to reopen and oil prices to come down.
insights.quote: He does not want to get into another fight with this country.
insights.locator: So, I mean, really, it’s the exception that proves the rule

insights.claim: Bremmer is 'pretty low confidence' that a broader U.S.-Iran nuclear deal will be reached, because unlike the multilateral JCPOA, the current MOU is a unilateral U.S.-Iran arrangement, and Iran can negotiate leverage through Oman, Qatar, the Saudis, Europeans, Indians, and Chinese while demanding maximum sanctions relief the U.S. is reluctant to grant.
insights.speaker: Ian Bremmer
insights.so_what: A specific, low-confidence call on nuclear-deal completion odds — informs sanctions-relief and Iran-oil-supply-return timelines for energy-market positioning.
insights.locator: So I’m pretty low confidence that we’re going to end up with an agreement

insights.claim: Iran retains indirect leverage over Israel via its influence on Hezbollah, and Bremmer thinks it 'very likely' that Israel-Lebanon fighting flares up again, though the U.S. will be reluctant to be drawn into a full renewed war with Iran over it.
insights.speaker: Ian Bremmer
insights.so_what: Flags Lebanon/Hezbollah as the most likely near-term flashpoint for renewed Middle East escalation, distinct from and more probable than a full U.S.-Iran war restart.
insights.locator: So I do think it’s plausible

insights.claim: Bremmer scores China's handling of the post-Iran-war moment 'probably a six out of ten,' arguing Beijing is forgoing clear opportunities — declining to lead Iran peace mediation (despite assembling a five-point plan with Pakistan) and not reaching out to a Japan and South Korea newly worried about U.S. reliability — in favor of cautious, incremental 'no-regret' moves.
insights.speaker: Ian Bremmer
insights.so_what: Counters the narrative of China as the primary strategic beneficiary of U.S. missteps — Bremmer argues Beijing's risk-aversion is actually leaving diplomatic opportunity on the table, a nuance relevant to China-influence forecasting.
insights.quote: I’d give them probably a six out of ten.
insights.locator: How do you think China is playing this moment

insights.claim: China's economy is being propped up almost entirely by manufacturing exports, producing trade surpluses so large that they are pushing Europe toward a trade war with China rather than closer alignment, despite the opening created by U.S. unpredictability.
insights.speaker: Ian Bremmer
insights.so_what: Undercuts the thesis that erratic U.S. policy automatically pushes allies toward China — Chinese export overcapacity is generating its own friction with Europe that offsets any U.S.-driven realignment.
insights.quote: the trade surpluses are wild and massive
insights.locator: I couldn’t agree more, but the only part of the Chinese economy

insights.claim: Bremmer notes China's capacity for abrupt policy pivots — citing the roughly three-day reversal of its 'zero COVID' policy — as a reason not to rule out a sudden future shift away from its current cautious, incremental economic posture, even though he does not expect one within the next one to two years.
insights.speaker: Ian Bremmer
insights.so_what: A calibration point for China-policy-shift risk: history shows Beijing can reverse entrenched policy within days once costs become undeniable, which argues against assuming permanent Chinese economic-reform paralysis.
insights.quote: zero COVID
insights.locator: But what was interesting, Dan, and we should remember this

themes.theme: Middle powers and a fractured order

insights.claim: Mark Carney's push for a consolidated 'middle powers' strategy has been undercut by G-Zero reality: no unified bloc has emerged, and countries instead pursue 'variable geometry,' hedging selectively issue by issue with no coherent common alignment.
insights.speaker: Ian Bremmer
insights.so_what: Signals that investors should not expect a coherent 'middle power bloc' trade or policy alignment to materialize — hedging behavior will remain fragmented and country-specific.
insights.quote: variable geometry
insights.locator: So Mark, first of all, I think that there have been three different speeches

insights.claim: Emmanuel Macron's framing of three structurally imbalanced economic policies — the EU systematically underinvesting, the U.S. taking on too much debt, and China systematically overproducing for export — would require trilateral coordination that none of the three major economic powers is currently willing to undertake.
insights.speaker: Ian Bremmer
insights.so_what: Frames persistent global macro imbalances (EU underinvestment, U.S. debt, Chinese export overcapacity) as structurally unresolved because no power will unilaterally adjust — relevant to long-run global rebalancing and currency/trade-policy forecasts.
insights.locator: That would be a wonderful conversation for all three leaders to have

insights.claim: G-7 tech CEOs sought a U.S.-led AI governance coalition at the G-7 summit and got nothing, so companies are 'writing their own rules' — Bremmer's clearest visual evidence for his 'technopolar' thesis that tech firms now act as functional sovereigns.
insights.speaker: Ian Bremmer
insights.so_what: Establishes that AI governance is currently a corporate self-regulation vacuum rather than a state-led framework — a structural risk factor for any AI-safety or AI-regulation-driven investment thesis.
insights.locator: You and I saw the G-7 and saw those CEOs

insights.claim: The Trump administration abruptly shut down Anthropic's most recent AI model over unaddressed safety risks after Senator Mark Warner revealed that internal NSA and other defenses were broken in an internal usage of the model, referred to in the conversation as 'Mythos.'
insights.speaker: Ian Bremmer
insights.so_what: A concrete, recent instance of AI capability outpacing both corporate safety controls and government oversight — directly relevant to AI-regulation risk for frontier-model developers and any AI-national-security policy debate.
insights.quote: all of the internal defenses for the NSA and others were broken when they were in an internal usage by Mythos
insights.locator: You saw Mark Warner talking about the fact

insights.claim: Bremmer names four AI-driven systemic risks he rates as giving him as much 'systemic pause' over the next one to three years as a cornered, nuclear-armed Putin: AI-enabled bioweapons threats from individuals and non-state actors, lethal autonomous drone swarms, AI-driven cyberattacks on critical/national-security infrastructure and power grids, and bot/agent fleets disrupting financial markets.
insights.speaker: Ian Bremmer
insights.so_what: A concrete, ranked risk framework for AI-driven tail risks over a specific near-term horizon — useful checklist for scenario-planning around AI-safety incidents, defense-tech, and critical-infrastructure cybersecurity exposure.
insights.locator: Dan, I can think of five risks like that from AI

insights.claim: Bremmer argues there is no real AI governance mechanism analogous to nuclear arms control's checks and balances — the closest current precedent, Treasury Secretary Bessent convening bankers on AI risk, excluded critical-infrastructure agencies like CISA and international partners, illustrating the gap.
insights.speaker: Ian Bremmer
insights.so_what: Identifies a specific institutional gap (no cross-sector, international AI-risk coordination body) that argues for skepticism toward any near-term comprehensive AI-safety regulatory regime.
insights.quote: Bessent with all the bankers
insights.locator: The only thing you had close to that was Bessent with all the bankers

insights.claim: Bremmer predicts that, absent proactive governance, a mass-casualty AI/drone incident — such as a large drone swarm attack killing hundreds or thousands at an open-air stadium — is the likely 'Cuban Missile Crisis' equivalent needed to force serious AI governance action.
insights.speaker: Ian Bremmer
insights.so_what: A specific scenario-based forecast for what type of shock event would catalyze binding AI regulation — a concrete marker for tracking whether AI governance remains reactive versus proactive.
insights.locator: I think that any of these sorts of attacks

themes.theme: Corruption as an analytic variable in Trump-era policy

insights.claim: Bremmer argues the U.S. has become a 'coin-operated democracy' where billions of dollars flow into elections and lobbying at a scale beyond any other advanced economy, producing a two-tier justice and legal system that shaped Trump's rise.
insights.speaker: Ian Bremmer
insights.so_what: Frames domestic corruption/money-in-politics as a root structural cause of Trump-era populism, relevant to forecasting the durability and successor movements of U.S. political realignment.
insights.quote: a coin-operated democracy
insights.locator: The U.S. has become a coin-operated democracy

insights.claim: Bremmer states that assessing Trump's domestic or foreign policy now requires tracking 'who is getting paid' as an independent variable, arguing Trump has shifted from a 'drain the swamp' posture in his first term to actively monetizing power in his second.
insights.speaker: Ian Bremmer
insights.so_what: A direct analytic methodology recommendation: any Trump-policy analysis (tariffs, sanctions, mineral deals, personnel appointments) should be screened for who financially benefits, not just stated policy rationale.
insights.quote: I don’t think you can assess Trump policies domestically or foreign policy unless you have as one of your independent variables who is getting paid.
insights.locator: I don’t think you can assess Trump policies

insights.claim: Bremmer predicts the next major U.S. political realignment is more likely to be a legitimate anti-corruption backlash than a repeat of Trump-style populism, since Trump's 'political revolution' has succeeded on border security but failed on ending wars, draining the swamp, or the Epstein files.
insights.speaker: Ian Bremmer
insights.so_what: A forward-looking political forecast: positions anti-corruption/anti-oligarch sentiment as the more likely next wave of U.S. populist politics, a variable relevant to future regulatory and tax-policy risk on concentrated wealth.
insights.quote: next political revolution in the U.S. is much more likely to be a legitimate anticorruption push
insights.locator: I think that the next political revolution

insights.claim: Of Trump's three defining traits — an authoritarian impulse, personal tolerance for corruption, and policy incompetence — Bremmer says the incompetence has the greatest real-world impact, since Trump routinely overrides more capable advisors (Rubio, Bessent, Ratcliffe, Caine), producing errors ranging from a reflecting pool to the Iran war.
insights.speaker: Ian Bremmer
insights.so_what: Identifies decision-making process (overriding competent advisors) rather than ideology as the mechanism behind Trump's biggest policy failures — a useful lens for anticipating future high-stakes foreign-policy misjudgments.
insights.quote: policy incompetence
insights.locator: And that of the three, I thought the one that had the greatest impact
